Hello, and welcome to the Never Spit the Difference Beyond the Book Virtual Course. The purpose of this course is to get you on track so you will be awesome. You get some key additional ideas we've been using since the book came out to supplement, amplify, and go into even more depth and exercise the concepts from the book. The book only includes 10 separate courses with over 50 chapters and multiple exercise scenarios. Each of the 10 courses correspond to a chapter from the book and will essentially follow the same general structure. We will introduce a subject for each course and define key concepts, terms, and techniques. Some of the courses will have a guided example which we will work through together in order to see how these skills can be used in real life situations. These interactive questions are designed to keep your attention and keep you thinking critically about how to use the skills from the book in real life situations. The courses will end with a summary to recap the most important points and at the end there will be an interactive scenario where you will have to answer questions in order to successfully negotiate your desired outcome. Just as in the book, every course will build upon the other. This course will begin with the basics of negotiation and a little bit of history as to how I learned the skills I did. Let's get started. The idea of negotiating for hostages has existed for ages as no coincidence that the word is a derivation of the word host. However, the way we think about it in today's terms is relatively new. There were swatins before there were ever hostage negotiators and they used to show up and say surrender or die. This was dumb. It violated autonomy. And it's the beginning of where we begin to see the parallel universe of hostage negotiations and your negotiations. What's the intersection between these two? Human nature. So this approach only works where human beings are involved. The first book of any real impact was Getting to Yes. This book changed the way everybody thought about negotiation. Their approach was basically that the emotional brain could be overcome by rational and joint problem-solving mindset. A whole world, including the FBI and NYPD, grabbed a copy. Around the same time, two Israeli psychologists Amos Tversky and Daniel Kahneman had a different theory, prospect theory. Prospect theory won the Nobel Prize in Economics for showing that man is actually a very irrational and emotional beast. Were humans, not cyborgs or volkens? Through their research, they proved that all humans suffer from cognitive bias. These brain processes literally distort the way we see the world. They discovered more than 150 of these. Please click on the options to learn a couple of the most important cognitive biases we run into.
